questions.title: Extracting Backbone Atom Coordinates
questions.description: Given a protein represented as an array of all-atom coordinates with atom names, extract only the backbone atoms (N, CA, C) for each residue. The input has shape (num_residues, num_atoms_per_residue, 3) with a corresponding atom name list.
questions.info: Protein backbone geometry is defined by three heavy atoms per residue: nitrogen (N), alpha-carbon (CA), and carbonyl carbon (C). These atoms define the peptide chain and are sufficient to reconstruct the backbone trace. AlphaFold2 (Jumper et al., Nature 2021) predicts per-residue frames anchored to these three backbone atoms, then builds full all-atom structures from torsion angles. MDAnalysis (Michaud-Agrawal et al., J Comput Chem 2011) provides utilities for selecting backbone atoms from molecular dynamics trajectories. Understanding backbone extraction is fundamental to all downstream structural analysis. Reference: Jumper et al., Nature 2021; Michaud-Agrawal et al., J Comput Chem 2011.
questions.code:
import numpy as np

# all_coords shape: (num_residues, num_atoms_per_residue, 3)
# atom_names: list of atom name strings, e.g. ['N', 'CA', 'C', 'O', 'CB', ...]
all_coords = np.random.randn(100, 14, 3)  # 100 residues, up to 14 atoms each
atom_names = ['N', 'CA', 'C', 'O', 'CB', 'CG', 'CG1', 'CG2', 'CD', 'CD1', 'CD2', 'CE', 'NZ', 'OG']

backbone_indices = [atom_names.index(a) for a in {{b1}}]
backbone_coords = all_coords[:, {{b2}}, :]
print('Backbone shape:', backbone_coords.shape)  # (100, 3, 3)

blanks.hint: The three backbone heavy atoms per residue are N, CA, and C.
Use the list of backbone atom indices to select from the atom dimension.

questions.title: Computing Backbone Torsion (Dihedral) Angles
questions.description: Compute the dihedral angle defined by four consecutive 3D points. Dihedral angles (phi, psi, omega) characterize protein backbone conformation. Given four points p0, p1, p2, p3, compute the angle between the planes (p0,p1,p2) and (p1,p2,p3).
questions.info: Backbone torsion angles (phi, psi, omega) are the primary degrees of freedom describing protein backbone conformation and are central to the Ramachandran plot. AlphaFold2 (Jumper et al., Nature 2021) predicts these torsion angles as part of its structure module to build all-atom coordinates from backbone frames. The dihedral angle is computed via cross products of bond vectors forming two planes, then taking the atan2 of their sine and cosine components. Boltzmann generators (Noe et al., Science 2019) operate in internal coordinate space including torsion angles to sample conformational ensembles. Proper handling of angle periodicity is critical for loss functions in structure prediction. Reference: Jumper et al., Nature 2021; Noe et al., Science 2019.
questions.code:
import numpy as np

def dihedral_angle(p0, p1, p2, p3):
    """Compute dihedral angle in radians for four 3D points."""
    b0 = p0 - p1
    b1 = {{b1}}
    b2 = p3 - p2
    # Normalize b1
    b1 = b1 / np.linalg.norm(b1)
    # Compute normal vectors to the two planes
    n1 = np.cross(b0, b1)
    n2 = {{b2}}
    # Compute the signed angle
    m1 = np.cross(n1, b1)
    x = np.dot(n1, n2)
    y = np.dot(m1, n2)
    return {{b3}}

# Test with known points
p0 = np.array([1.0, 0.0, 0.0])
p1 = np.array([0.0, 0.0, 0.0])
p2 = np.array([0.0, 1.0, 0.0])
p3 = np.array([0.0, 1.0, 1.0])
angle = dihedral_angle(p0, p1, p2, p3)
print(f'Dihedral angle: {np.degrees(angle):.1f} degrees')

blanks.hint: The central bond vector goes from p1 to p2.
The second plane normal is the cross product of b1 and b2.
Use atan2 with the sine and cosine components to get the signed angle.

questions.title: SE(3) Rotation via Rotation Matrix
questions.description: Apply an SE(3) transformation (rotation + translation) to a set of 3D protein coordinates. SE(3) is the special Euclidean group in 3D, combining SO(3) rotations with translations.
questions.info: SE(3)-equivariance is a fundamental symmetry for molecular learning: the output of a model should transform consistently when the input is rotated or translated. SE(3)-Transformers (Fuchs et al., NeurIPS 2020) introduced attention mechanisms that are equivariant to rotations and translations by operating on geometric features in irreducible representations. The SE(3) group acts on coordinates as x' = Rx + t where R is a rotation matrix in SO(3) and t is a translation vector. This is the foundation for equivariant neural networks used in protein structure prediction. Maintaining equivariance eliminates the need for data augmentation over rotations. Reference: Fuchs et al., NeurIPS 2020; Thomas et al., arXiv 2018.
questions.code:
import numpy as np

def se3_transform(coords, R, t):
    """Apply SE(3) transformation to coordinates.
    coords: (N, 3) array of 3D points
    R: (3, 3) rotation matrix
    t: (3,) translation vector
    Returns: (N, 3) transformed coordinates
    """
    return {{b1}}

# Create a rotation matrix (90 degrees around z-axis)
theta = np.pi / 2
R = np.array([[np.cos(theta), -np.sin(theta), 0],
              [{{b2}}],
              [0, 0, 1]])
t = np.array([1.0, 2.0, 3.0])

coords = np.random.randn(50, 3)
transformed = se3_transform(coords, R, t)
print('Original centroid:', coords.mean(axis=0))
print('Transformed centroid:', transformed.mean(axis=0))

The second row of a z-axis rotation matrix is [sin(theta), cos(theta), 0].

questions.title: Geometric Vector Perceptron - Scalar & Vector Features
questions.description: Implement the core GVP operation that processes both scalar features (rotation-invariant) and vector features (rotation-equivariant). The GVP maps (s, V) -> (s', V') where s are scalars and V are 3D vectors.
questions.info: Geometric Vector Perceptrons (Jing et al., ICLR 2021) introduce a simple yet powerful architecture for learning on 3D protein structures by maintaining separate scalar (invariant) and vector (equivariant) feature channels. The key insight is that vector norms are rotation-invariant and can be mixed with scalar features, while vector directions are updated via learned linear maps that preserve equivariance. GVP-GNN achieves strong performance on protein structure tasks including model quality assessment and function prediction. The architecture avoids expensive higher-order tensor operations used in other equivariant networks while retaining geometric expressiveness. Reference: Jing et al., ICLR 2021; Jing et al., NeurIPS 2021.
questions.code:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn

class GVP(nn.Module):
    """Geometric Vector Perceptron.
    Processes scalar features s (B, s_in) and
    vector features V (B, v_in, 3).
    """
    def __init__(self, s_in, s_out, v_in, v_out):
        super().__init__()
        self.v_out = v_out
        # Linear map on vector features
        self.Wv = nn.Linear(v_in, v_out, bias=False)
        # Scalar MLP takes s_in + v_in (from vector norms)
        self.Ws = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Linear(s_in + v_in, s_out),
            nn.ReLU()
        )

    def forward(self, s, V):
        # V: (B, v_in, 3) -> transpose for linear -> (B, 3, v_in)
        Vt = V.transpose(-1, -2)
        # Apply learned linear to vector channels
        Vh = self.Wv(Vt).transpose(-1, -2)  # (B, v_out, 3)
        # Compute rotation-invariant vector norms
        v_norms = {{b1}}  # (B, v_in)
        # Concatenate scalar features with vector norms
        s_combined = {{b2}}
        s_out = self.Ws(s_combined)
        # Normalize output vectors
        Vh_norms = torch.norm(Vh, dim=-1, keepdim=True).clamp(min=1e-6)
        V_out = Vh * (torch.sigmoid(Vh_norms) / {{b3}})
        return s_out, V_out

gvp = GVP(s_in=64, s_out=64, v_in=16, v_out=16)
s = torch.randn(8, 64)
V = torch.randn(8, 16, 3)
s_out, V_out = gvp(s, V)
print('Scalar out:', s_out.shape, 'Vector out:', V_out.shape)

blanks.hint: Compute the L2 norm of each vector along the last (xyz) dimension.
Concatenate the original scalar features with the rotation-invariant vector norms.
Divide by the clamped norms to normalize, then scale by sigmoid gating.

questions.title: Residue Frame Representation (Rotation + Translation)
questions.description: Construct a local coordinate frame for each residue from three backbone atoms (N, CA, C). The frame consists of a rotation matrix (3x3) and a translation vector (the CA position), following the Gram-Schmidt process used in AlphaFold2.
questions.info: AlphaFold2 (Jumper et al., Nature 2021) represents each residue as a rigid-body frame (rotation + translation) anchored at the CA atom, with axes defined by the N-CA and CA-C bond directions via Gram-Schmidt orthogonalization. This frame representation is central to the Invariant Point Attention (IPA) mechanism, which operates on points expressed in local residue frames to achieve SE(3)-invariance. The rotation matrix columns define the local x, y, z axes of each residue. Frame representations enable composing local and global coordinate transformations efficiently. Reference: Jumper et al., Nature 2021; Ingraham et al., NeurIPS 2019.
questions.code:
import numpy as np

def build_residue_frames(N_pos, CA_pos, C_pos):
    """Build local frames for each residue.
    N_pos, CA_pos, C_pos: (L, 3) backbone atom positions
    Returns: rotations (L, 3, 3), translations (L, 3)
    """
    # Translation is the CA position
    translations = {{b1}}
    # First axis: CA -> C direction (normalized)
    v1 = C_pos - CA_pos
    e1 = v1 / np.linalg.norm(v1, axis=-1, keepdims=True)
    # Second vector: CA -> N direction
    v2 = N_pos - CA_pos
    # Gram-Schmidt: remove component along e1
    u2 = v2 - np.sum(v2 * e1, axis=-1, keepdims=True) * e1
    e2 = u2 / np.linalg.norm(u2, axis=-1, keepdims=True)
    # Third axis: cross product
    e3 = {{b2}}
    # Stack into rotation matrices (L, 3, 3)
    rotations = {{b3}}
    return rotations, translations

L = 50
N_pos = np.random.randn(L, 3)
CA_pos = np.random.randn(L, 3)
C_pos = np.random.randn(L, 3)
R, t = build_residue_frames(N_pos, CA_pos, C_pos)
print('Rotations:', R.shape, 'Translations:', t.shape)
# Verify orthogonality
print('R @ R^T ~= I:', np.allclose(R[0] @ R[0].T, np.eye(3), atol=1e-6))

blanks.hint: The translation (origin) of the residue frame is the alpha-carbon position.
The third orthonormal axis is the cross product of the first two.
Stack the three orthonormal vectors as columns to form the rotation matrix.

questions.title: RMSD Computation Between Two Structures
questions.description: Compute the Root Mean Square Deviation (RMSD) between two sets of aligned 3D coordinates. RMSD is the standard metric for comparing protein structures.
questions.info: RMSD (Root Mean Square Deviation) is the most widely used metric for quantifying structural similarity between two protein conformations after optimal superposition. It is defined as sqrt(mean(sum_of_squared_distances)) over corresponding atoms. AlphaFold2 (Jumper et al., Nature 2021) reports GDT and lDDT scores but RMSD remains ubiquitous in structural biology. MDAnalysis (Michaud-Agrawal et al., J Comput Chem 2011) provides efficient RMSD implementations for trajectory analysis. Low RMSD (<2 Angstroms for CA atoms) generally indicates high structural similarity. RMSD is sensitive to outlier residues, motivating alternatives like GDT-TS for structure prediction benchmarking. Reference: Jumper et al., Nature 2021; Michaud-Agrawal et al., J Comput Chem 2011.
questions.code:

questions.title: Kabsch Alignment Algorithm
questions.description: Implement the Kabsch algorithm to find the optimal rotation matrix that minimizes RMSD between two sets of 3D coordinates. This requires centering both point sets, computing the cross-covariance matrix, and performing SVD.
questions.info: The Kabsch algorithm (Kabsch, Acta Crystallogr 1976) finds the optimal rotation to superimpose two point sets by minimizing RMSD. It works by centering both structures, computing the cross-covariance matrix H = P^T Q, performing SVD to get H = USV^T, then constructing R = V diag(1,1,sign(det(VU^T))) U^T to ensure a proper rotation. This algorithm is fundamental to structural bioinformatics and is used extensively in tools like PyMOL and MDAnalysis (Michaud-Agrawal et al., J Comput Chem 2011). AlphaFold2 (Jumper et al., Nature 2021) uses a differentiable version for loss computation during training. Reference: Kabsch, Acta Crystallogr 1976; Jumper et al., Nature 2021.
questions.code:
import numpy as np

def kabsch_align(P, Q):
    """Find optimal rotation to align P onto Q.
    P, Q: (N, 3) coordinate arrays
    Returns: R (3,3) rotation matrix, t (3,) translation
    """
    # Center both point sets
    p_center = P.mean(axis=0)
    q_center = Q.mean(axis=0)
    P_centered = P - p_center
    Q_centered = Q - q_center
    # Cross-covariance matrix
    H = {{b1}}
    # SVD
    U, S, Vt = np.linalg.svd(H)
    # Correct for reflection
    d = np.sign(np.linalg.det({{b2}}))
    sign_matrix = np.diag([1, 1, d])
    # Optimal rotation
    R = {{b3}}
    # Translation
    t = q_center - R @ p_center
    return R, t

# Test: apply known rotation and recover it
P = np.random.randn(50, 3)
theta = np.pi / 4
R_true = np.array([[np.cos(theta), -np.sin(theta), 0],
                   [np.sin(theta),  np.cos(theta), 0],
                   [0, 0, 1]])
Q = (R_true @ P.T).T + np.array([5, 3, 1])
R_est, t_est = kabsch_align(P, Q)
print('Rotation error:', np.linalg.norm(R_est - R_true))

blanks.hint: The cross-covariance matrix is H = P_centered^T @ Q_centered.
Check the sign of det(V @ U^T) to ensure a proper rotation (no reflection).
R = V @ diag(1,1,d) @ U^T ensures a proper rotation.

questions.title: Contact Map from Coordinates
questions.description: Compute a binary contact map from CA (alpha-carbon) coordinates. Two residues are in contact if their CA-CA distance is below a threshold (typically 8 Angstroms).
questions.info: Contact maps are a coarse-grained representation of protein 3D structure that encode which residue pairs are spatially close. They have been widely used as intermediate targets in protein structure prediction, notably in early deep learning approaches before end-to-end coordinate prediction (Senior et al., Nature 2020). AlphaFold2 (Jumper et al., Nature 2021) predicts full distance distributions (distograms) rather than binary contacts. Contact maps are rotation and translation invariant, making them useful for structure comparison. The typical CA-CA distance threshold of 8 Angstroms captures side-chain mediated interactions. Reference: Senior et al., Nature 2020; Jumper et al., Nature 2021.
questions.code:
import numpy as np

def compute_contact_map(ca_coords, threshold=8.0):
    """Compute binary contact map from CA coordinates.
    ca_coords: (L, 3) array of CA positions
    threshold: distance cutoff in Angstroms
    Returns: (L, L) binary contact matrix
    """
    # Compute pairwise distance matrix
    diff = ca_coords[:, None, :] - {{b1}}  # (L, L, 3)
    dist_matrix = {{b2}}  # (L, L)
    contact_map = (dist_matrix < {{b3}}).astype(np.float32)
    return contact_map

ca_coords = np.random.randn(80, 3) * 10  # 80 residues
cmap = compute_contact_map(ca_coords)
print('Contact map shape:', cmap.shape)
print('Number of contacts:', int(cmap.sum()))
print('Is symmetric:', np.allclose(cmap, cmap.T))

Take the norm along the last axis (the xyz dimension) to get distances.
Compare distances against the threshold parameter to get binary contacts.

questions.title: Radius of Gyration
questions.description: Compute the radius of gyration (Rg) of a protein, which measures the compactness of a structure. Rg is defined as the RMS distance of all atoms from the center of mass.
questions.info: The radius of gyration (Rg) quantifies the overall compactness or spread of a molecular structure and is commonly used to monitor protein folding and unfolding in molecular dynamics simulations. It is defined as Rg = sqrt(mean(|r_i - r_centroid|^2)). MDAnalysis (Michaud-Agrawal et al., J Comput Chem 2011) and OpenMM (Eastman et al., PLoS Comput Biol 2017) provide built-in Rg calculations. A decrease in Rg during simulation typically indicates compaction or folding. Rg can also be measured experimentally via small-angle X-ray scattering (SAXS). Boltzmann generators (Noe et al., Science 2019) can sample conformations with varying Rg to explore the folding landscape. Reference: Michaud-Agrawal et al., J Comput Chem 2011; Eastman et al., PLoS Comput Biol 2017.
questions.code:
import numpy as np

questions.title: Pairwise Distance Matrix (Distogram)
questions.description: Compute the full pairwise distance matrix between all CA atoms. Distance matrices (distograms) are a fundamental representation used in protein structure prediction.
questions.info: Distograms, or discretized pairwise distance distributions, are a key representation in modern protein structure prediction. AlphaFold2 (Jumper et al., Nature 2021) predicts binned distance distributions between all residue pairs as part of its Evoformer output. The pairwise distance matrix is SE(3)-invariant, meaning it is unchanged by rotations and translations, making it a natural target for prediction from sequence. Distance matrix representations were central to AlphaFold1 (Senior et al., Nature 2020), which predicted distance distributions and then optimized coordinates to match them. SchNet (Schutt et al., NeurIPS 2017) uses pairwise distances as input features for continuous-filter convolutions. Reference: Jumper et al., Nature 2021; Schutt et al., NeurIPS 2017.

questions.title: Protein Graph Construction (k-Nearest Neighbors)
questions.description: Construct a k-nearest-neighbor graph from protein CA coordinates. Each residue becomes a node, and edges connect each residue to its k closest spatial neighbors. This graph is used as input to GNNs for protein structure learning.
questions.info: Graph-based representations of proteins enable the use of Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) for structure-based tasks. GVP-GNN (Jing et al., ICLR 2021) constructs k-nearest-neighbor graphs from CA coordinates where k is typically 10-30. Each node represents a residue with features like amino acid type, and edge features encode relative positions and distances. The kNN graph captures local spatial context without requiring a fixed distance cutoff, adapting to varying local packing densities. SchNet (Schutt et al., NeurIPS 2017) uses continuous radial filters on distance-based graphs. This approach has proven effective for protein design, model quality assessment, and function prediction. Reference: Jing et al., ICLR 2021; Schutt et al., NeurIPS 2017.
questions.code:
import numpy as np

def build_knn_graph(coords, k=10):
    """Build kNN graph from CA coordinates.
    coords: (L, 3) array
    k: number of nearest neighbors
    Returns: edge_index (2, num_edges), edge_vectors (num_edges, 3)
    """
    L = coords.shape[0]
    # Pairwise distances
    diff = coords[:, None, :] - coords[None, :, :]
    dist = np.linalg.norm(diff, axis=-1)  # (L, L)
    # Set self-distance to infinity
    np.fill_diagonal(dist, {{b1}})
    # Get k nearest neighbors for each node
    knn_indices = np.argsort(dist, axis=1)[:, {{b2}}]  # (L, k)
    # Build edge index
    src = np.repeat(np.arange(L), k)
    dst = {{b3}}
    edge_index = np.stack([src, dst], axis=0)  # (2, L*k)
    edge_vectors = coords[dst] - coords[src]
    return edge_index, edge_vectors

coords = np.random.randn(100, 3) * 10
edge_index, edge_vecs = build_knn_graph(coords, k=10)
print('Edge index shape:', edge_index.shape)   # (2, 1000)
print('Edge vectors shape:', edge_vecs.shape)   # (1000, 3)

blanks.hint: Set self-distances to infinity so a node is not its own neighbor.
After sorting, take the first k indices to get the k nearest neighbors.
Flatten the kNN indices to create the destination node array.

questions.title: Protein Graph Construction (Radius-Based)
questions.description: Construct a radius-based graph from CA coordinates. Edges connect all residue pairs within a distance cutoff. This complements kNN graphs by enforcing a physical distance constraint.
questions.info: Radius-based graphs connect all atom pairs within a spatial cutoff, capturing physically meaningful interactions. Unlike kNN graphs, radius graphs guarantee that all edges represent contacts within a defined distance, which is important for modeling physical interactions like van der Waals forces or hydrogen bonds. SchNet (Schutt et al., NeurIPS 2017) uses a distance cutoff to define the molecular graph for its continuous-filter convolution layers. DimeNet (Gasteiger et al., ICLR 2020) extends this with directional message passing on radius graphs. In protein structure prediction, AlphaFold-Multimer (Evans et al., bioRxiv 2022) uses distance-based cropping during training. A typical cutoff of 10-12 Angstroms captures most non-bonded interactions. Reference: Schutt et al., NeurIPS 2017; Evans et al., bioRxiv 2022.

questions.title: Radial Basis Function Distance Encoding
questions.description: Encode scalar pairwise distances into a vector of radial basis function (RBF) features. RBF encoding is used in geometric GNNs to expand distances into a higher-dimensional, smooth representation.
questions.info: Radial basis function (RBF) expansion converts scalar distances into a multi-dimensional feature vector using a set of Gaussian functions centered at different distances. This is a key component of SchNet (Schutt et al., NeurIPS 2017), which uses RBF-expanded distances as input to continuous-filter convolutional layers. The expansion provides a smooth, differentiable representation that helps neural networks learn distance-dependent interactions. DimeNet (Gasteiger et al., ICLR 2020) and GemNet also use RBF expansions. The centers are typically evenly spaced from 0 to a maximum distance, with a fixed width parameter controlling overlap between basis functions. Reference: Schutt et al., NeurIPS 2017; Gasteiger et al., ICLR 2020.

questions.title: Invariant Point Attention - Point Projection
questions.description: Implement the point projection step of Invariant Point Attention (IPA) from AlphaFold2. Given query points in local residue frames, transform them to global coordinates using the residue rotation and translation.
questions.info: Invariant Point Attention (IPA) is a central component of AlphaFold2's structure module (Jumper et al., Nature 2021). IPA achieves SE(3)-invariance by projecting learned query/key/value points from local residue frames into the global frame, computing attention based on both these projected points and pair features. The key operation transforms local points p_local to global coordinates via p_global = R @ p_local + t, where R and t are the residue's rotation and translation. This enables the model to reason about 3D geometry while maintaining invariance to the overall orientation of the protein. The attention weights combine Euclidean distances between projected points with sequence-based pair features. Reference: Jumper et al., Nature 2021.
questions.code:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn

def project_points_to_global(points_local, rotations, translations):
    """Project points from local residue frames to global coordinates.
    points_local: (B, L, n_pts, 3) points in local frames
    rotations: (B, L, 3, 3) rotation matrices per residue
    translations: (B, L, 3) translation vectors per residue
    Returns: (B, L, n_pts, 3) points in global frame
    """
    # Apply rotation: R @ p for each point
    # rotations: (B, L, 3, 3), points_local: (B, L, n_pts, 3)
    # We need (B, L, 3, 3) @ (B, L, 3, n_pts) -> (B, L, 3, n_pts)
    pts_transposed = points_local.transpose(-1, -2)  # (B, L, 3, n_pts)
    rotated = {{b1}}  # (B, L, 3, n_pts)
    rotated = rotated.transpose(-1, -2)  # (B, L, n_pts, 3)
    # Apply translation
    global_pts = rotated + {{b2}}
    return global_pts

B, L, n_pts = 2, 50, 4
points_local = torch.randn(B, L, n_pts, 3)
# Random valid rotations (identity for simplicity)
rotations = torch.eye(3).unsqueeze(0).unsqueeze(0).expand(B, L, 3, 3).clone()
translations = torch.randn(B, L, 3)
global_pts = project_points_to_global(points_local, rotations, translations)
print('Global points shape:', global_pts.shape)
# With identity rotation, global = local + translation
expected = points_local + {{b3}}
print('Identity rotation check:', torch.allclose(global_pts, expected, atol=1e-5))

questions.title: lDDT Score Computation
questions.description: Implement the local Distance Difference Test (lDDT), a superposition-free score for assessing structural predictions. lDDT measures the fraction of inter-residue distances preserved within thresholds.
questions.info: lDDT (local Distance Difference Test) is a per-residue structure quality metric that does not require superposition, making it robust to domain movements (Mariani et al., Bioinformatics 2013). AlphaFold2 (Jumper et al., Nature 2021) predicts per-residue lDDT (pLDDT) as a confidence score, which has become a standard measure of prediction reliability. lDDT computes the fraction of inter-residue distances in the prediction that are within tolerance thresholds (0.5, 1, 2, 4 Angstroms) of the true distances, considering only residue pairs within 15 Angstroms in the reference structure. The score ranges from 0 to 1, with >0.9 indicating very high accuracy. Its locality makes it informative for identifying well-predicted and poorly-predicted regions. Reference: Mariani et al., Bioinformatics 2013; Jumper et al., Nature 2021.
questions.code:
import numpy as np

def compute_lddt(pred_coords, true_coords, cutoff=15.0,
                 thresholds=[0.5, 1.0, 2.0, 4.0]):
    """Compute lDDT score.
    pred_coords, true_coords: (L, 3) CA coordinates
    Returns: per-residue lDDT scores (L,)
    """
    L = true_coords.shape[0]
    # Compute distance matrices
    true_dists = np.linalg.norm(
        true_coords[:, None] - true_coords[None, :], axis=-1)  # (L, L)
    pred_dists = np.linalg.norm(
        pred_coords[:, None] - pred_coords[None, :], axis=-1)
    # Mask: only consider pairs within cutoff in the TRUE structure
    mask = (true_dists < cutoff) & ({{b1}})
    # Distance differences
    dist_diff = {{b2}}  # (L, L)
    # For each threshold, compute fraction preserved
    scores = np.zeros(L)
    for i in range(L):
        neighbors = mask[i]
        if neighbors.sum() == 0:
            scores[i] = 0
            continue
        diffs_i = dist_diff[i, neighbors]
        preserved = np.mean([{{b3}} for t in thresholds])
        scores[i] = preserved
    return scores

np.random.seed(42)
true_coords = np.random.randn(50, 3) * 10
pred_coords = true_coords + np.random.randn(50, 3) * 0.5  # small perturbation
lddt = compute_lddt(pred_coords, true_coords)
print(f'Mean lDDT: {lddt.mean():.3f}')  # Should be high for small perturbation

questions.title: SE(3)-Equivariant Message Passing
questions.description: Implement a simple SE(3)-equivariant message passing layer. Messages combine scalar (invariant) and vector (equivariant) information along edges of a protein graph.
questions.info: SE(3)-equivariant message passing ensures that vector outputs rotate consistently with input rotations, while scalar outputs remain invariant. SE(3)-Transformers (Fuchs et al., NeurIPS 2020) achieve this by passing messages that separately update scalar and vector channels. The key principle is that scalar features can depend on rotation-invariant quantities (distances, dot products), while vector updates must be linear combinations of input vectors to preserve equivariance. EGNN (Satorras et al., ICML 2021) provides a simpler equivariant GNN using coordinate updates weighted by learned edge functions. GVP-GNN (Jing et al., ICLR 2021) extends this with Geometric Vector Perceptrons. Reference: Fuchs et al., NeurIPS 2020; Satorras et al., ICML 2021.
questions.code:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn

class EquivariantMessagePass(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, scalar_dim=64):
        super().__init__()
        # Scalar message MLP (invariant): takes scalar features + distance
        self.scalar_mlp = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Linear(2 * scalar_dim + 1, scalar_dim),
            nn.ReLU(),
            nn.Linear(scalar_dim, scalar_dim)
        )
        # Weight for vector message (equivariant)
        self.vec_weight = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Linear(2 * scalar_dim + 1, scalar_dim),
            nn.ReLU(),
            nn.Linear(scalar_dim, 1)
        )

    def forward(self, s, x, edge_index):
        """s: (N, scalar_dim) scalar features
           x: (N, 3) coordinates
           edge_index: (2, E) edges
        """
        src, dst = edge_index
        # Edge vectors and distances (invariant)
        rel_vec = x[dst] - x[src]  # (E, 3)
        dist = {{b1}}  # (E, 1)
        # Scalar message
        scalar_input = torch.cat([s[src], s[dst], dist], dim=-1)
        scalar_msg = self.scalar_mlp(scalar_input)  # (E, scalar_dim)
        # Vector message weight (scalar, so invariant)
        w = self.vec_weight(scalar_input)  # (E, 1)
        vec_msg = {{b2}}  # (E, 3): weighted direction vectors
        # Aggregate messages (sum)
        s_new = torch.zeros_like(s)
        x_update = torch.zeros_like(x)
        s_new.index_add_(0, {{b3}}, scalar_msg)
        x_update.index_add_(0, dst, vec_msg)
        return s + s_new, x + x_update

N, E = 50, 200
s = torch.randn(N, 64)
x = torch.randn(N, 3)
edge_index = torch.randint(0, N, (2, E))
model = EquivariantMessagePass()
s_new, x_new = model(s, x, edge_index)
print('Updated scalar:', s_new.shape, 'Updated coords:', x_new.shape)

blanks.hint: Compute the Euclidean distance (norm of the relative vector) as an invariant feature.
Scale the direction vector by the learned scalar weight to keep equivariance.
Aggregate messages at destination nodes using index_add with the dst indices.

questions.title: Molecular Docking Scoring Function
questions.description: Implement a simple physics-inspired docking scoring function that combines van der Waals and electrostatic terms. This scores the quality of a protein-ligand binding pose.
questions.info: Molecular docking scoring functions estimate the binding affinity of a ligand in a protein binding pocket. Classical scoring functions combine van der Waals (Lennard-Jones potential), electrostatic (Coulomb), and desolvation terms. DiffDock (Corso et al., ICLR 2023) replaces handcrafted scoring with a diffusion generative model that learns to generate binding poses directly, significantly outperforming traditional docking. AutoDock Vina (Trott & Olson, J Comput Chem 2010) remains a widely used empirical scoring function. The van der Waals term captures steric complementarity, while the electrostatic term models charge-charge interactions. Machine learning scoring functions increasingly complement physics-based approaches. Reference: Corso et al., ICLR 2023; Trott & Olson, J Comput Chem 2010.
questions.code:
import numpy as np

def docking_score(protein_coords, ligand_coords,
                  protein_charges, ligand_charges,
                  epsilon=1.0, sigma=3.5, coulomb_k=332.0):
    """Simple docking score: vdW (Lennard-Jones) + electrostatic.
    protein_coords: (Np, 3), ligand_coords: (Nl, 3)
    protein_charges, ligand_charges: (Np,), (Nl,) partial charges
    Returns: total score (scalar)
    """
    # Pairwise distances between protein and ligand atoms
    diff = protein_coords[:, None, :] - ligand_coords[None, :, :]
    dist = np.linalg.norm(diff, axis=-1)  # (Np, Nl)
    dist = np.clip(dist, 1e-3, None)  # avoid division by zero
    # Lennard-Jones 12-6 potential
    r_ratio = sigma / dist
    vdw = 4 * epsilon * ({{b1}} - {{b2}})
    # Coulomb electrostatic
    q_prod = {{b3}}  # (Np, Nl)
    electrostatic = coulomb_k * q_prod / dist
    total = vdw.sum() + electrostatic.sum()
    return total

np.random.seed(0)
prot_coords = np.random.randn(100, 3) * 5
lig_coords = np.random.randn(20, 3) * 2 + np.array([5, 0, 0])
prot_q = np.random.randn(100) * 0.1
lig_q = np.random.randn(20) * 0.1
score = docking_score(prot_coords, lig_coords, prot_q, lig_q)
print(f'Docking score: {score:.2f}')

The attractive term of the Lennard-Jones potential is (sigma/r)^6.

questions.title: Boltzmann Distribution Sampling Weight
questions.description: Compute Boltzmann weights from energy values to weight conformational samples. The Boltzmann distribution p(x) is proportional to exp(-E(x)/kT), fundamental to statistical mechanics of proteins.
questions.info: The Boltzmann distribution relates the probability of a molecular conformation to its energy: p(x) proportional to exp(-E(x)/kT). Boltzmann generators (Noe et al., Science 2019) use normalizing flows to learn a bijective mapping from a simple prior to the Boltzmann distribution of molecular systems, enabling efficient sampling of equilibrium conformations. This approach circumvents the time-scale problem of conventional molecular dynamics simulations. OpenMM (Eastman et al., PLoS Comput Biol 2017) provides energy computation for molecular systems with various force fields. Proper normalization of Boltzmann weights is critical to avoid numerical overflow, typically using the log-sum-exp trick. Reference: Noe et al., Science 2019; Eastman et al., PLoS Comput Biol 2017.
questions.code:
import numpy as np

def boltzmann_weights(energies, temperature=300.0, kB=0.001987):
    """Compute normalized Boltzmann weights from energies.
    energies: (N,) array of energies in kcal/mol
    temperature: in Kelvin
    kB: Boltzmann constant in kcal/(mol*K)
    Returns: (N,) normalized probability weights
    """
    kT = kB * temperature
    # Compute log weights (use log-sum-exp for numerical stability)
    log_weights = {{b1}}
    # Normalize using log-sum-exp trick
    log_weights_shifted = log_weights - {{b2}}
    weights = np.exp(log_weights_shifted)
    weights = weights / {{b3}}
    return weights

# Test: lower energy conformations should have higher weight
energies = np.array([-10.0, -5.0, 0.0, 5.0, 10.0])
weights = boltzmann_weights(energies)
print('Weights:', weights)
print('Sum:', weights.sum())  # Should be 1.0
print('Lowest energy has highest weight:', np.argmax(weights) == 0)

blanks.hint: Log Boltzmann weight is -E / (kB * T).
Subtract the maximum log weight for numerical stability (log-sum-exp trick).
Normalize by dividing by the sum so that weights form a probability distribution.

questions.title: GVP Edge Feature Construction
questions.description: Construct edge features for a GVP-GNN protein graph. Each edge has scalar features (distance, sequence separation) and a vector feature (unit direction vector from source to destination).
questions.info: GVP-GNN (Jing et al., ICLR 2021) constructs protein graphs with both scalar and vector features on edges. Edge scalar features typically include the pairwise distance and sequence separation (how far apart residues are in the primary sequence). The edge vector feature is the unit direction vector between connected residues, which is equivariant under rotation. These features allow the network to reason about both distance-based and direction-based geometric relationships. The model uses separate scalar and vector message passing channels to maintain equivariance. This edge featurization has been adopted in many subsequent protein structure models including ESM-IF (Hsu et al., ICML 2022). Reference: Jing et al., ICLR 2021; Hsu et al., ICML 2022.
questions.code:
import numpy as np

def build_gvp_edge_features(coords, edge_index, seq_len):
    """Build scalar and vector edge features for GVP-GNN.
    coords: (L, 3) CA coordinates
    edge_index: (2, E) source-destination pairs
    seq_len: length of the protein sequence
    Returns: edge_scalars (E, 2), edge_vectors (E, 1, 3)
    """
    src, dst = edge_index
    # Direction vectors
    rel_vec = coords[dst] - coords[src]  # (E, 3)
    dist = np.linalg.norm(rel_vec, axis=-1, keepdims=True)  # (E, 1)
    # Unit direction vector (equivariant vector feature)
    unit_vec = {{b1}}  # (E, 3)
    # Sequence separation (invariant scalar feature)
    seq_sep = {{b2}}  # (E,)
    seq_sep_encoded = seq_sep.astype(np.float32) / seq_len  # normalize
    # Edge scalar features: [distance, normalized sequence separation]
    edge_scalars = np.concatenate([dist, seq_sep_encoded[:, None]], axis=-1)  # (E, 2)
    # Edge vector features: unit direction, shape (E, 1, 3)
    edge_vectors = {{b3}}
    return edge_scalars, edge_vectors

L = 100
coords = np.random.randn(L, 3) * 10
# Simple sequential edges
src = np.arange(L - 1)
dst = np.arange(1, L)
edge_index = np.stack([src, dst])
scalars, vectors = build_gvp_edge_features(coords, edge_index, L)
print('Edge scalars:', scalars.shape)   # (99, 2)
print('Edge vectors:', vectors.shape)   # (99, 1, 3)
print('Unit vectors normalized:', np.allclose(np.linalg.norm(vectors, axis=-1), 1.0))

Sequence separation is the absolute difference of residue indices.

questions.title: SchNet Continuous-Filter Convolution
questions.description: Implement the continuous-filter convolution from SchNet, which generates convolutional filters from interatomic distances via an MLP applied to RBF-expanded distances.
questions.info: SchNet (Schutt et al., NeurIPS 2017) introduced continuous-filter convolutions for molecular property prediction, where filter weights are generated as a function of interatomic distance rather than being fixed on a grid. Distances are first expanded into RBF features, then passed through an MLP to produce per-edge filter weights. The message for each edge is the element-wise product of the neighbor's features with the generated filter. This approach naturally handles the continuous and irregular structure of molecular graphs. SchNet achieves strong results on QM9 and MD17 benchmarks and inspired many subsequent architectures including PaiNN and DimeNet. Reference: Schutt et al., NeurIPS 2017; Schutt et al., J Chem Phys 2018.
questions.code:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import numpy as np

class SchNetInteraction(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, hidden_dim=128, num_rbf=20):
        super().__init__()
        self.num_rbf = num_rbf
        # Atom-wise linear
        self.atom_linear = nn.Linear(hidden_dim, hidden_dim)
        # Continuous filter: RBF -> filter weights
        self.filter_net = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Linear(num_rbf, hidden_dim),
            nn.SiLU(),
            nn.Linear(hidden_dim, hidden_dim)
        )
        self.output_linear = nn.Linear(hidden_dim, hidden_dim)

    def rbf_expand(self, dist, num_rbf=20, cutoff=10.0):
        centers = torch.linspace(0, cutoff, num_rbf, device=dist.device)
        width = cutoff / num_rbf
        return torch.exp(-0.5 * ((dist.unsqueeze(-1) - centers) / width) ** 2)

    def forward(self, h, edge_index, distances):
        """h: (N, hidden_dim), edge_index: (2, E), distances: (E,)"""
        src, dst = edge_index
        # Transform neighbor features
        h_neighbors = self.atom_linear(h[src])  # (E, hidden_dim)
        # Generate filters from distances
        rbf = {{b1}}  # (E, num_rbf)
        W = {{b2}}  # (E, hidden_dim)
        # Element-wise product: features * filter
        messages = h_neighbors * W  # (E, hidden_dim)
        # Aggregate
        out = torch.zeros_like(h)
        out.index_add_(0, {{b3}}, messages)
        return h + self.output_linear(out)

N, E, hidden = 50, 300, 128
h = torch.randn(N, hidden)
edge_index = torch.randint(0, N, (2, E))
dist = torch.rand(E) * 10
model = SchNetInteraction(hidden)
h_new = model(h, edge_index, dist)
print('Output shape:', h_new.shape)

Pass the RBF features through the filter-generating network.

questions.title: RMSD Over an MD Trajectory
questions.description: Given a molecular dynamics trajectory stored as a 3D array of shape (T, N, 3) where T is the number of frames and N is the number of CA atoms, compute the RMSD of each frame relative to the first frame (reference). This is a standard trajectory analysis task.
questions.info: Tracking RMSD over time is one of the most fundamental analyses performed on molecular dynamics trajectories. It reveals whether a simulation has equilibrated and quantifies how far the protein deviates from a reference structure. MDAnalysis (Michaud-Agrawal et al., J Comput Chem 2011) provides efficient RMSD computation routines for large trajectories. A plateau in the RMSD time series typically indicates that the simulation has reached equilibrium. OpenMM (Eastman et al., PLoS Comput Biol 2017) is a popular engine for running MD simulations that produce such trajectories. Reference: Michaud-Agrawal et al., J Comput Chem 2011; Eastman et al., PLoS Comput Biol 2017.

    diff = traj - {{b1}}  # (T, N, 3)
    sq_dist = np.sum(diff ** 2, axis={{b2}})  # (T, N)
    return np.sqrt({{b3}})

# Test

questions.title: Root Mean Square Fluctuation (RMSF)
questions.description: Compute the per-residue Root Mean Square Fluctuation (RMSF) from an MD trajectory. RMSF measures the average deviation of each atom from its mean position over the trajectory and is analogous to crystallographic B-factors.
questions.info: RMSF quantifies the flexibility of each residue over the course of an MD simulation and correlates with experimental B-factors from X-ray crystallography. High RMSF values indicate flexible loops or termini, while low values correspond to well-packed core regions. RMSF is computed as sqrt(mean((r_i(t) - <r_i>)^2)) for each atom i, where <r_i> is its time-averaged position. MDAnalysis (Michaud-Agrawal et al., J Comput Chem 2011) provides built-in RMSF calculations. AlphaFold2's pLDDT scores (Jumper et al., Nature 2021) anticorrelate with RMSF, as disordered regions tend to have low pLDDT and high RMSF. Reference: Michaud-Agrawal et al., J Comput Chem 2011; Jumper et al., Nature 2021.

import numpy as np

def compute_rmsf(traj):
    """Compute per-residue RMSF from a trajectory.
    traj: (T, N, 3) trajectory array
    Returns: (N,) RMSF values
    """
    mean_pos = {{b1}}  # (N, 3)
    deviations = traj - mean_pos[None, :, :]  # (T, N, 3)
    sq_dev = np.sum(deviations ** 2, axis={{b2}})  # (T, N)
    return np.sqrt({{b3}})

np.random.seed(0)
T, N = 1000, 80
# Simulate trajectory: residues 30-40 are flexible
traj = np.random.randn(T, N, 3) * 0.1
traj[:, 30:40, :] += np.random.randn(T, 10, 3) * 2.0  # extra flexibility
rmsf = compute_rmsf(traj)
print('RMSF shape:', rmsf.shape)
print('Mean RMSF (rigid):', rmsf[:30].mean())
print('Mean RMSF (flexible):', rmsf[30:40].mean())

questions.title: Time-Lagged Independent Component Analysis (TICA)
questions.description: Implement the core TICA computation: given a trajectory featurized as a (T, D) matrix, compute the time-lagged covariance matrices C(0) and C(tau), then solve the generalized eigenvalue problem to find the slowest collective motions.
questions.info: TICA (Perez-Hernandez et al., J Chem Phys 2013) identifies the slowest relaxation modes in molecular dynamics data by solving a generalized eigenvalue problem on time-lagged covariance matrices. It is a key dimensionality reduction method for constructing Markov state models (MSMs) of protein dynamics. The time-lagged covariance matrix C(tau) captures correlations between features at time t and t+tau, and the eigenvectors with the largest eigenvalues correspond to the slowest processes. TICA is implemented in PyEMMA (Scherer et al., J Chem Theory Comput 2015) and deeptime. Unlike PCA, which finds directions of maximal variance, TICA finds directions that decorrelate most slowly. Reference: Perez-Hernandez et al., J Chem Phys 2013; Scherer et al., J Chem Theory Comput 2015.
questions.code:
import numpy as np
from scipy.linalg import eigh

def tica(features, lag=10, n_components=2):
    """Time-lagged Independent Component Analysis.
    features: (T, D) feature trajectory
    lag: time lag in frames
    n_components: number of slow modes to extract
    Returns: eigenvalues (n_components,), eigenvectors (D, n_components)
    """
    T, D = features.shape
    # Center the data
    features = features - features.mean(axis=0)
    # Instantaneous covariance C(0)
    C0 = {{b1}} / (T - 1)
    # Time-lagged covariance C(tau)
    X_t = features[:T - lag]
    X_t_tau = features[{{b2}}:]
    C_tau = X_t.T @ X_t_tau / (T - lag - 1)
    # Symmetrize C_tau
    C_tau = 0.5 * (C_tau + C_tau.T)
    # Solve generalized eigenvalue problem: C_tau v = lambda C0 v
    eigenvalues, eigenvectors = eigh(C_tau, {{b3}},
                                      subset_by_index=[D - n_components, D - 1])
    # Sort by descending eigenvalue (slowest modes first)
    idx = np.argsort(eigenvalues)[::-1]
    return eigenvalues[idx], eigenvectors[:, idx]

np.random.seed(42)
T, D = 5000, 20
features = np.random.randn(T, D)
evals, evecs = tica(features, lag=10, n_components=2)
print('TICA eigenvalues:', evals)
print('Eigenvector shape:', evecs.shape)  # (20, 2)

blanks.hint: The instantaneous covariance is X^T X / (T-1) for centered data.
The time-lagged data starts at index 'lag' and goes to the end.

questions.title: Markov State Model Transition Matrix
questions.description: Given a discrete state trajectory (sequence of cluster assignments from an MD trajectory), estimate the row-stochastic transition matrix by counting transitions and normalizing each row. This is the foundation of Markov state models.
questions.info: Markov state models (MSMs) discretize the conformational space into metastable states and model dynamics as memoryless transitions between them (Chodera & Noe, Curr Opin Struct Biol 2014). The transition matrix T(tau) is estimated by counting transitions between states at lag time tau and row-normalizing. MSMs enable extraction of kinetic information (relaxation timescales, rate constants) from MD data and are widely used in protein folding studies. PyEMMA (Scherer et al., J Chem Theory Comput 2015) and deeptime provide robust MSM estimation. The implied timescales from the eigenvalues of T should plateau at the chosen lag time for the Markov assumption to hold. Reference: Chodera & Noe, Curr Opin Struct Biol 2014; Scherer et al., J Chem Theory Comput 2015.
questions.code:
import numpy as np

def estimate_transition_matrix(dtraj, n_states, lag=1):
    """Estimate MSM transition matrix from a discrete trajectory.
    dtraj: (T,) array of integer state labels
    n_states: number of discrete states
    lag: lag time in frames
    Returns: (n_states, n_states) row-stochastic transition matrix
    """
    count_matrix = np.zeros((n_states, n_states))
    for t in range(len(dtraj) - lag):
        i = dtraj[t]
        j = dtraj[{{b1}}]
        count_matrix[i, j] += 1
    # Row-normalize to get transition probabilities
    row_sums = {{b2}}
    row_sums[row_sums == 0] = 1  # avoid division by zero
    T_mat = count_matrix / {{b3}}
    return T_mat

np.random.seed(0)
# Simulate a 3-state trajectory
n_states = 3
dtraj = np.random.choice(n_states, size=10000)
T_mat = estimate_transition_matrix(dtraj, n_states, lag=1)
print('Transition matrix:\n', T_mat)
print('Row sums:', T_mat.sum(axis=1))  # Should all be 1.0

Sum each row to get the total outgoing transitions from each state.
Reshape row_sums to (n_states, 1) for broadcasting during division.

questions.title: MSM Implied Timescales from Eigenvalues
questions.description: Compute the implied timescales of a Markov state model from the eigenvalues of its transition matrix. The implied timescales characterize the relaxation rates of the slowest dynamical processes.
questions.info: The implied timescales of an MSM are computed as t_i = -tau / ln(lambda_i) where lambda_i are the eigenvalues of the transition matrix and tau is the lag time (Chodera & Noe, Curr Opin Struct Biol 2014). The largest eigenvalue is always 1 (corresponding to the stationary distribution), and the remaining eigenvalues encode relaxation timescales. These timescales should be independent of the lag time if the Markov assumption is satisfied, providing a validation criterion for MSMs. PyEMMA (Scherer et al., J Chem Theory Comput 2015) uses implied timescale plots as a primary diagnostic. The slowest timescale corresponds to the rate-limiting conformational change. Reference: Chodera & Noe, Curr Opin Struct Biol 2014; Scherer et al., J Chem Theory Comput 2015.
questions.code:
import numpy as np

def implied_timescales(T_mat, lag_time=1.0):
    """Compute implied timescales from transition matrix.
    T_mat: (K, K) row-stochastic transition matrix
    lag_time: lag time in physical units
    Returns: sorted array of implied timescales (excluding stationary)
    """
    eigenvalues = np.linalg.eigvals(T_mat)
    eigenvalues = np.real(eigenvalues)
    # Sort descending
    eigenvalues = np.sort(eigenvalues)[::-1]
    # Exclude the stationary eigenvalue (lambda ~= 1)
    lambdas = eigenvalues[{{b1}}:]
    # Filter out non-positive eigenvalues
    lambdas = lambdas[lambdas > 0]
    timescales = {{b2}}
    return np.sort(timescales)[::-1]

# Test with a simple 3-state transition matrix
T = np.array([[0.9, 0.05, 0.05],
              [0.05, 0.85, 0.10],
              [0.05, 0.10, 0.85]])
lag = 1.0  # 1 ns
ts = implied_timescales(T, lag_time=lag)
print('Implied timescales (ns):', ts)
print('Number of timescales:', len(ts))  # Should be {{b3}}

blanks.hint: Skip the first (largest) eigenvalue which is ~1 for the stationary distribution.
Implied timescale is t = -tau / ln(lambda) for each eigenvalue.
For a K-state MSM, there are K-1 implied timescales (excluding the stationary mode).

questions.title: Diffusion Map Embedding
questions.description: Implement the diffusion map algorithm: given pairwise distances between conformations, construct a Gaussian kernel, normalize it to form a diffusion operator, and extract the leading eigenvectors as a low-dimensional embedding.
questions.info: Diffusion maps (Coifman & Lafon, Appl Comput Harmon Anal 2006) provide a nonlinear dimensionality reduction that captures the intrinsic geometry of data manifolds and is widely used for MD trajectory analysis. The algorithm constructs a Gaussian kernel from pairwise distances, normalizes it to approximate the Laplace-Beltrami operator, and uses the leading eigenvectors as coordinates. Unlike PCA, diffusion maps capture nonlinear slow modes and are related to the transfer operator of the dynamics. Rohrdanz et al. (J Chem Phys 2011) applied diffusion maps to protein folding trajectories. The bandwidth parameter epsilon controls the locality of the kernel and should be chosen relative to the data scale. Reference: Coifman & Lafon, Appl Comput Harmon Anal 2006; Rohrdanz et al., J Chem Phys 2011.
questions.code:
import numpy as np

def diffusion_map(dist_matrix, epsilon=1.0, n_components=2):
    """Compute diffusion map embedding.
    dist_matrix: (N, N) pairwise distance matrix
    epsilon: Gaussian kernel bandwidth
    n_components: number of embedding dimensions
    Returns: (N, n_components) embedding coordinates
    """
    # Gaussian kernel
    K = np.exp(-dist_matrix ** 2 / {{b1}})
    # Normalize: form the diffusion operator
    D = np.diag({{b2}})
    D_inv = np.diag(1.0 / np.diag(D))
    P = D_inv @ K  # row-stochastic diffusion matrix
    # Eigendecomposition
    eigenvalues, eigenvectors = np.linalg.eigh(P)
    # Sort descending
    idx = np.argsort(eigenvalues)[::-1]
    eigenvalues = eigenvalues[idx]
    eigenvectors = eigenvectors[:, idx]
    # Skip the trivial first eigenvector, take next n_components
    embedding = eigenvectors[:, {{b3}}]
    return embedding

np.random.seed(42)
N = 200
# Simulate 2D manifold data
t = np.linspace(0, 2 * np.pi, N)
data = np.column_stack([np.cos(t), np.sin(t), 0.1 * np.random.randn(N)])
dist_mat = np.linalg.norm(data[:, None] - data[None, :], axis=-1)
embed = diffusion_map(dist_mat, epsilon=0.5, n_components=2)
print('Embedding shape:', embed.shape)  # (200, 2)

blanks.hint: The Gaussian kernel uses exp(-d^2 / (2 * epsilon)) as the bandwidth normalization.
The degree matrix D has diagonal entries equal to the row sums of K.
Skip the first trivial eigenvector and take the next n_components eigenvectors.

questions.title: Free Energy Surface from Histogram
questions.description: Compute a 2D free energy surface from projected trajectory data (e.g., TICA or PCA coordinates) using the Boltzmann inversion: F = -kT * ln(P), where P is the probability density estimated via a 2D histogram.
questions.info: Free energy surfaces (FES) provide a thermodynamic landscape of molecular conformations and are routinely computed from MD simulation data projected onto collective variables. The Boltzmann inversion F = -kT * ln(P(x)) converts a probability density P(x) into a free energy, revealing metastable states as minima and transition states as barriers. Histogram-based estimation is the simplest approach, though kernel density estimation is often more accurate (Noe et al., Science 2019). Free energy surfaces projected onto TICA coordinates (Perez-Hernandez et al., J Chem Phys 2013) are widely used in MSM workflows. Bins with zero counts are typically assigned infinite free energy or masked out. Reference: Noe et al., Science 2019; Perez-Hernandez et al., J Chem Phys 2013.
questions.code:
import numpy as np

def free_energy_surface(x, y, bins=50, temperature=300.0, kB=0.001987):
    """Compute 2D free energy surface via histogram.
    x, y: (T,) projected coordinates
    Returns: free_energy (bins, bins), xedges, yedges
    """
    kT = kB * temperature
    hist, xedges, yedges = np.histogram2d(x, y, bins=bins, density=True)
    # Avoid log(0) by masking zero-count bins
    hist[hist == 0] = {{b1}}
    free_energy = {{b2}} * np.log(hist)
    # Shift so minimum free energy is zero
    free_energy -= {{b3}}
    return free_energy, xedges, yedges

np.random.seed(42)
# Simulate bimodal distribution (two metastable states)
T = 50000
state = np.random.choice([0, 1], size=T, p=[0.6, 0.4])
x = np.random.randn(T) + 3 * state
y = np.random.randn(T) + 2 * state
FE, xe, ye = free_energy_surface(x, y, bins=40)
print('FE shape:', FE.shape)
print('Min FE:', FE.min())  # Should be ~0

blanks.hint: Replace zero counts with a tiny positive number to avoid log(0).
The Boltzmann inversion is F = -kT * ln(P).
Shift the surface so that the global minimum is at zero free energy.

questions.title: Metadynamics Gaussian Bias Potential
questions.description: Implement the bias potential accumulation step of metadynamics: deposit Gaussian hills along a 1D collective variable to discourage revisiting previously sampled states, thereby enhancing sampling of rare events.
questions.info: Metadynamics (Laio & Parrinello, Proc Natl Acad Sci 2002) enhances sampling by periodically adding Gaussian bias potentials along collective variables (CVs), filling free energy minima and encouraging the system to explore new conformations. The accumulated bias V(s, t) = sum of Gaussians centered at previously visited CV values converges to the negative of the free energy surface. Well-tempered metadynamics (Barducci et al., Phys Rev Lett 2008) reduces the Gaussian height over time for better convergence. PLUMED (Tribello et al., Comput Phys Commun 2014) is the standard software for running metadynamics with various MD engines. The method is widely used to study protein folding, ligand binding, and conformational transitions. Reference: Laio & Parrinello, Proc Natl Acad Sci 2002; Barducci et al., Phys Rev Lett 2008.
questions.code:
import numpy as np

def accumulate_metad_bias(cv_values, height=1.0, sigma=0.5):
    """Compute the metadynamics bias from deposited Gaussians.
    cv_values: (T,) collective variable values at each deposition step
    Returns: function that evaluates the bias at any CV value
    """
    centers = cv_values.copy()  # Gaussian centers

    def bias_potential(s):
        """Evaluate bias at collective variable value(s) s."""
        s = np.atleast_1d(s)
        V = np.zeros_like(s, dtype=float)
        for c in centers:
            V += height * np.exp(-0.5 * ((s - c) / {{b1}}) ** 2)
        return V

    return bias_potential

# Simulate CV values deposited during metadynamics
np.random.seed(42)
cv_traj = np.concatenate([np.random.randn(50) - 2, np.random.randn(50) + 2])
bias_fn = accumulate_metad_bias(cv_traj, height=0.5, sigma=0.3)

# Evaluate bias on a grid
s_grid = np.linspace(-5, 5, 200)
bias_values = bias_fn(s_grid)
print('Bias shape:', bias_values.shape)
print('Max bias at:', s_grid[{{b2}}])  # Should be near the most visited CV value
print('Bias is non-negative:', np.all(bias_values >= {{b3}}))

blanks.hint: The Gaussian width parameter sigma controls the spread of each deposited hill.
Find the grid point with the highest accumulated bias.
Gaussian hills are always non-negative, so the total bias is always >= 0.

questions.title: Normal Mode Analysis - Hessian Eigendecomposition
questions.description: Given a simplified Hessian matrix (second derivative of the potential energy with respect to atomic displacements), compute the normal modes and their frequencies via eigendecomposition. Normal modes describe the collective vibrational motions of a protein.
questions.info: Normal mode analysis (NMA) computes the harmonic vibrational modes of a protein around its energy minimum by diagonalizing the mass-weighted Hessian matrix (Go et al., Proc Natl Acad Sci 1983). The lowest-frequency non-trivial modes (after removing 6 rigid-body modes) often correspond to functionally relevant large-scale motions like domain opening/closing. The eigenvalues give squared frequencies (omega^2) and the eigenvectors give displacement patterns. ProDy (Bakan et al., Bioinformatics 2011) provides efficient NMA implementations for proteins. NMA is computationally much cheaper than MD and provides insight into the softest deformation modes accessible to the protein. Reference: Go et al., Proc Natl Acad Sci 1983; Bakan et al., Bioinformatics 2011.
questions.code:
import numpy as np

def normal_mode_analysis(hessian, n_modes=10):
    """Compute normal modes from the Hessian matrix.
    hessian: (3N, 3N) second derivative matrix
    n_modes: number of non-trivial modes to return
    Returns: frequencies (n_modes,), mode_vectors (3N, n_modes)
    """
    eigenvalues, eigenvectors = {{b1}}
    # Sort by ascending eigenvalue
    idx = np.argsort(eigenvalues)
    eigenvalues = eigenvalues[idx]
    eigenvectors = eigenvectors[:, idx]
    # Skip the first 6 trivial modes (3 translation + 3 rotation)
    nontrivial_evals = eigenvalues[{{b2}}:6 + n_modes]
    nontrivial_evecs = eigenvectors[:, 6:6 + n_modes]
    # Frequencies: omega = sqrt(|eigenvalue|)
    frequencies = np.sqrt(np.abs(nontrivial_evals))
    return frequencies, nontrivial_evecs

# Create a simple test Hessian (spring network)
N_atoms = 20
dim = 3 * N_atoms
np.random.seed(42)
A = np.random.randn(dim, dim)
hessian = {{b3}}  # Ensure symmetric positive semi-definite
freqs, modes = normal_mode_analysis(hessian, n_modes=10)
print('Frequencies:', freqs[:5])
print('Mode shapes:', modes.shape)  # (60, 10)

blanks.hint: Use eigh for symmetric matrices to get eigenvalues and eigenvectors.
The first 6 eigenvalues correspond to rigid-body translation and rotation.
A^T A is guaranteed to be symmetric positive semi-definite.

questions.title: Gaussian Network Model - Kirchhoff Matrix
questions.description: Construct the Kirchhoff (connectivity) matrix for a Gaussian Network Model (GNM) from CA coordinates. The GNM uses a simple distance cutoff to define contacts and models protein dynamics with a single-parameter elastic network.
questions.info: The Gaussian Network Model (Bahar et al., Fold Des 1997) is a coarse-grained elastic network model that represents each residue as a node connected by harmonic springs to neighbors within a cutoff distance (typically 7-10 Angstroms). The Kirchhoff matrix Gamma has off-diagonal elements of -1 for contacts and diagonal elements equal to the node's degree. The pseudo-inverse of Gamma gives the covariance matrix of residue fluctuations, and the slowest modes predict experimental B-factors remarkably well. ProDy (Bakan et al., Bioinformatics 2011) implements GNM and the related Anisotropic Network Model (ANM). GNM captures the topology-dependent aspects of protein dynamics without detailed force field parameters. Reference: Bahar et al., Fold Des 1997; Bakan et al., Bioinformatics 2011.
questions.code:
import numpy as np

def gnm_kirchhoff(ca_coords, cutoff=7.5):
    """Build the GNM Kirchhoff matrix.
    ca_coords: (N, 3) CA coordinates
    cutoff: distance cutoff in Angstroms
    Returns: (N, N) Kirchhoff matrix
    """
    N = ca_coords.shape[0]
    dist = np.linalg.norm(ca_coords[:, None] - ca_coords[None, :], axis=-1)
    # Contact matrix (exclude self)
    contacts = (dist < cutoff) & ({{b1}})
    # Build Kirchhoff matrix
    Gamma = -contacts.astype(float)
    np.fill_diagonal(Gamma, {{b2}})
    return Gamma

np.random.seed(42)
ca = np.random.randn(50, 3) * 5
Gamma = gnm_kirchhoff(ca, cutoff=7.5)
print('Kirchhoff shape:', Gamma.shape)
print('Row sums (should be ~0):', np.allclose(Gamma.sum(axis=1), {{b3}}))

blanks.hint: Exclude the diagonal (self-contacts) from the contact matrix.
Each diagonal element equals the number of contacts (degree) of that node.
Each row of the Kirchhoff matrix sums to zero by construction.

questions.title: Anisotropic Network Model - Hessian Construction
questions.description: Construct the 3Nx3N Hessian matrix for the Anisotropic Network Model (ANM). Unlike GNM which only models fluctuation magnitudes, ANM captures the directionality of motions by using 3D displacement vectors.
questions.info: The Anisotropic Network Model (Atilgan et al., Biophys J 2001) extends the GNM by accounting for the directionality of fluctuations. The ANM Hessian is a 3Nx3N matrix where each 3x3 block between contacting residues i and j is proportional to the outer product of the unit vector connecting them. The eigenvectors of the Hessian (after removing 6 rigid-body modes) give anisotropic displacement patterns that predict crystallographic B-factors and functional motions. ProDy (Bakan et al., Bioinformatics 2011) implements ANM with various options for spring constants. ANM has been applied to study allosteric communication, domain motions, and conformational transitions in proteins. Reference: Atilgan et al., Biophys J 2001; Bakan et al., Bioinformatics 2011.
questions.code:
import numpy as np

def anm_hessian(ca_coords, cutoff=15.0, gamma=1.0):
    """Build the ANM Hessian matrix.
    ca_coords: (N, 3) CA coordinates
    cutoff: distance cutoff in Angstroms
    gamma: spring constant
    Returns: (3N, 3N) Hessian matrix
    """
    N = ca_coords.shape[0]
    H = np.zeros((3 * N, 3 * N))
    for i in range(N):
        for j in range(i + 1, N):
            diff = ca_coords[j] - ca_coords[i]
            dist = np.linalg.norm(diff)
            if dist < cutoff:
                # Unit direction vector outer product
                e = diff / dist
                block = -gamma * {{b1}}  # 3x3 block
                # Off-diagonal blocks
                H[3*i:3*i+3, 3*j:3*j+3] = block
                H[3*j:3*j+3, {{b2}}] = block
                # Diagonal blocks
                H[3*i:3*i+3, 3*i:3*i+3] -= block
                H[3*j:3*j+3, 3*j:3*j+3] -= {{b3}}
    return H

np.random.seed(42)
ca = np.random.randn(20, 3) * 5
H = anm_hessian(ca, cutoff=10.0)
print('Hessian shape:', H.shape)  # (60, 60)
print('Symmetric:', np.allclose(H, H.T))
evals = np.linalg.eigvalsh(H)
print('Num zero eigenvalues (rigid body):', np.sum(np.abs(evals) < 1e-6))

blanks.hint: The 3x3 interaction block is the outer product of the unit direction vector with itself.
The (j, i) block is the same as the (i, j) block for this symmetric Hessian.
The diagonal blocks accumulate the negative sum of all off-diagonal blocks.

questions.title: B-Factors from GNM Pseudo-Inverse
questions.description: Predict crystallographic B-factors from a Gaussian Network Model. B-factors are proportional to the diagonal of the pseudo-inverse of the Kirchhoff matrix, which gives the mean-square fluctuations.
questions.info: Crystallographic B-factors (or temperature factors) quantify the mean-square displacement of atoms from their average positions and are recorded in PDB files. The GNM predicts B-factors as B_i = (8 * pi^2 / 3) * (kT / gamma) * [Gamma^{-1}]_ii, where Gamma is the Kirchhoff matrix (Bahar et al., Fold Des 1997). The pseudo-inverse is used because Gamma has a zero eigenvalue corresponding to overall translation. GNM B-factor predictions correlate remarkably well (r ~ 0.5-0.8) with experimental values despite the model's simplicity. AlphaFold2's pLDDT (Jumper et al., Nature 2021) also correlates with flexibility, providing a complementary prediction. Reference: Bahar et al., Fold Des 1997; Yang et al., Proc Natl Acad Sci 2009.
questions.code:
import numpy as np

def predict_bfactors(kirchhoff, temperature=300.0, gamma=1.0, kB=0.001987):
    """Predict B-factors from GNM Kirchhoff matrix.
    kirchhoff: (N, N) Kirchhoff matrix
    Returns: (N,) predicted B-factors
    """
    eigenvalues, eigenvectors = np.linalg.eigh(kirchhoff)
    # Use pseudo-inverse: skip the zero eigenvalue mode
    # Gamma_pinv = sum over non-zero modes: (1/lambda_k) * v_k @ v_k^T
    nonzero = np.abs(eigenvalues) > {{b1}}
    Gamma_pinv_diag = np.zeros(kirchhoff.shape[0])
    for k in range(len(eigenvalues)):
        if nonzero[k]:
            Gamma_pinv_diag += {{b2}} / eigenvalues[k]
    kT = kB * temperature
    bfactors = (8 * np.pi ** 2 / 3) * (kT / gamma) * {{b3}}
    return bfactors

np.random.seed(42)
ca = np.random.randn(40, 3) * 5
from numpy.linalg import norm
dist = norm(ca[:, None] - ca[None, :], axis=-1)
contacts = (dist < 7.5) & (dist > 0)
Gamma = -contacts.astype(float)
np.fill_diagonal(Gamma, contacts.sum(axis=1))
bfactors = predict_bfactors(Gamma)
print('B-factors shape:', bfactors.shape)
print('Mean B-factor:', bfactors.mean())

blanks.hint: Use a small threshold to identify non-zero eigenvalues for the pseudo-inverse.
The diagonal of v_k @ v_k^T is simply v_k^2 (element-wise square).
Multiply the prefactor by the diagonal of the pseudo-inverse of Gamma.

questions.title: Coarse-Grained Mapping Operator
questions.description: Implement a coarse-graining operator that maps all-atom protein coordinates to a reduced representation by averaging atom positions within each residue to produce a single bead per residue (center of mass mapping).
questions.info: Coarse-grained (CG) representations reduce the number of degrees of freedom in molecular simulations by grouping multiple atoms into single interaction sites (beads). The MARTINI force field (Marrink et al., J Phys Chem B 2007) uses roughly a 4:1 heavy-atom-to-bead mapping. The simplest CG mapping assigns one bead per residue at the center of mass. CG models enable simulation of larger systems over longer timescales while retaining essential physics. Machine learning has been used to learn optimal CG mappings and force fields (Wang et al., ACS Cent Sci 2019). The mapping operator M transforms all-atom coordinates to CG coordinates: R = M * r. Reference: Marrink et al., J Phys Chem B 2007; Wang et al., ACS Cent Sci 2019.
questions.code:
import numpy as np

def coarse_grain_com(all_atom_coords, residue_indices, masses=None):
    """Map all-atom coords to one bead per residue (center of mass).
    all_atom_coords: (N_atoms, 3)
    residue_indices: (N_atoms,) integer residue assignment for each atom
    masses: (N_atoms,) atomic masses (uniform if None)
    Returns: (N_residues, 3) CG bead positions
    """
    if masses is None:
        masses = np.ones(len(all_atom_coords))
    n_residues = {{b1}}
    cg_coords = np.zeros((n_residues, 3))
    for res_id in range(n_residues):
        mask = residue_indices == res_id
        w = masses[mask]
        cg_coords[res_id] = {{b2}} / {{b3}}
    return cg_coords

np.random.seed(42)
N_atoms = 500
N_res = 50
all_atom = np.random.randn(N_atoms, 3) * 10
res_idx = np.repeat(np.arange(N_res), N_atoms // N_res)
cg = coarse_grain_com(all_atom, res_idx)
print('CG coords shape:', cg.shape)  # (50, 3)
print('Reduction ratio:', N_atoms / N_res)

blanks.hint: The number of CG beads equals the number of unique residue indices.
Sum the mass-weighted coordinates for atoms in this residue.
Divide by the total mass of the residue to get the center of mass.

questions.title: MARTINI-Style Bead Assignment
questions.description: Implement a simplified MARTINI-like coarse-graining that assigns bead types based on amino acid chemical properties. Each amino acid is mapped to a backbone bead plus zero or more sidechain beads with polarity types (polar, nonpolar, charged, etc.).
questions.info: The MARTINI force field (Marrink et al., J Phys Chem B 2007) maps roughly four heavy atoms to one CG bead and assigns each bead a type reflecting its chemical nature: polar (P), nonpolar (N), apolar (C), or charged (Q). MARTINI has been widely used for lipid membrane simulations and protein-membrane interactions. MARTINI 3 (Souza et al., Nat Methods 2021) introduced refined bead types and improved protein secondary structure handling. The mapping of amino acids to bead types encodes essential chemistry: charged residues (Lys, Arg, Asp, Glu) get Q-type beads, hydrophobic residues (Ala, Val, Leu) get C-type beads. This enables simulation of systems with millions of effective atoms. Reference: Marrink et al., J Phys Chem B 2007; Souza et al., Nat Methods 2021.
questions.code:
import numpy as np

# Simplified MARTINI-like bead type mapping
BEAD_TYPES = {
    'ALA': ['BB', 'C1'], 'VAL': ['BB', 'C1'], 'LEU': ['BB', 'C1'],
    'ILE': ['BB', 'C1'], 'PHE': ['BB', 'C1', 'C1'],
    'GLY': ['BB'], 'PRO': ['BB', 'C1'],
    'SER': ['BB', 'P1'], 'THR': ['BB', 'P1'],
    'ASN': ['BB', 'P1'], 'GLN': ['BB', 'P1'],
    'ASP': ['BB', 'Q1'], 'GLU': ['BB', 'Q1'],
    'LYS': ['BB', 'Q1', 'C1'], 'ARG': ['BB', 'Q1'],
}

def count_cg_beads(sequence):
    """Count total CG beads for a protein sequence.
    sequence: list of 3-letter amino acid codes
    Returns: total number of CG beads
    """
    total = 0
    for aa in sequence:
        total += {{b1}}
    return total

def get_bead_type_counts(sequence):
    """Count bead types across the whole protein."""
    counts = {}
    for aa in sequence:
        for bead in BEAD_TYPES.get(aa, ['BB']):
            counts[bead] = counts.get(bead, 0) + {{b2}}
    return counts

sequence = ['ALA', 'GLY', 'LYS', 'ASP', 'PHE', 'SER', 'VAL', 'GLU', 'LEU', 'ARG']
n_beads = count_cg_beads(sequence)
n_atoms_approx = len(sequence) * {{b3}}  # rough all-atom count
print(f'Sequence length: {len(sequence)}')
print(f'CG beads: {n_beads}')
print(f'Approx all-atom: {n_atoms_approx}')
print(f'Reduction: {n_atoms_approx / n_beads:.1f}x')
print('Bead type counts:', get_bead_type_counts(sequence))

Increment the count for each bead type by 1.
A typical amino acid has roughly 10-14 heavy atoms on average.

questions.title: SchNet-Style Energy and Force Prediction
questions.description: Build a minimal SchNet-inspired model that predicts total energy from atomic positions and types, then obtains forces as the negative gradient of the energy with respect to positions using PyTorch autograd.
questions.info: SchNet (Schutt et al., NeurIPS 2017) predicts molecular energies as a sum of atomic contributions learned via continuous-filter convolutions on interatomic distances. Forces are computed as the negative gradient of the energy with respect to atomic positions, ensuring energy conservation by construction. This approach is central to ML force fields used in MD simulations. PaiNN (Schutt et al., ICML 2021) extended SchNet with equivariant message passing for improved accuracy. ML force fields achieve near-DFT accuracy at a fraction of the computational cost, enabling long-timescale simulations of proteins and materials. The energy-conserving force computation via autograd is a key architectural design principle. Reference: Schutt et al., NeurIPS 2017; Schutt et al., ICML 2021.
questions.code:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn

class SimpleMLForceField(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, n_atom_types=10, hidden_dim=64):
        super().__init__()
        self.embed = nn.Embedding(n_atom_types, hidden_dim)
        self.interaction = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Linear(hidden_dim + 16, hidden_dim),
            nn.SiLU(),
            nn.Linear(hidden_dim, hidden_dim)
        )
        self.output = nn.Linear(hidden_dim, 1)  # per-atom energy

    def rbf(self, dist, n=16, cutoff=5.0):
        centers = torch.linspace(0, cutoff, n, device=dist.device)
        return torch.exp(-0.5 * ((dist.unsqueeze(-1) - centers) / (cutoff / n)) ** 2)

    def forward(self, positions, atom_types):
        """positions: (N, 3), atom_types: (N,) LongTensor"""
        h = self.embed(atom_types)  # (N, hidden_dim)
        # Pairwise distances
        diff = positions.unsqueeze(0) - positions.unsqueeze(1)
        dist = torch.norm(diff, dim=-1)  # (N, N)
        rbf_feat = self.rbf(dist)  # (N, N, 16)
        # Simple aggregation
        rbf_sum = rbf_feat.mean(dim=1)  # (N, 16)
        h = self.interaction(torch.cat([h, rbf_sum], dim=-1))
        atomic_energies = self.output(h).squeeze(-1)  # (N,)
        total_energy = {{b1}}
        return total_energy

        self.out_vector_dim = out_vector_dim
        self.vector_dim = vector_dim

        return out_vectors

questions.title: Umbrella Sampling: Biased Histogram Combination
questions.description: Implement a simplified weighted histogram analysis method (WHAM) step. Given biased histograms from umbrella sampling windows along a collective variable, unbias them by subtracting the known harmonic bias potential to reconstruct the unbiased free energy.

WHAM (Kumar et al., J Comput Chem 1992) combines data from all windows to reconstruct the unbiased free energy surface by solving self-consistent equations that account for the overlap between windows. Each window i applies a bias V_i(s) = 0.5 * k * (s - s_i)^2 where s_i is the window center and k is the spring constant. Modern alternatives include MBAR (Shirts & Chodera, J Chem Phys 2008) which provides statistically optimal estimates. Reference: Kumar et al., J Comput Chem 1992; Shirts & Chodera, J Chem Phys 2008.

import numpy as np

def simplified_wham(cv_samples, centers, k_spring, bins=50, temperature=300.0, kB=0.001987):

    cv_samples: list of arrays, CV samples from each window
    centers: (N_windows,) window centers
    k_spring: spring constant for all windows
    Returns: bin_centers, free_energy

    kT = kB * temperature
    all_samples = np.concatenate(cv_samples)
    bin_edges = np.linspace(all_samples.min(), all_samples.max(), bins + 1)
    bin_centers = 0.5 * (bin_edges[:-1] + bin_edges[1:])

        weights = np.exp(bias / kT)  # unbias by reweighting
        hist, _ = np.histogram(samples, bins=bin_edges, weights={{b2}})
        unbiased_hist += hist

    unbiased_hist[unbiased_hist == 0] = 1e-300
    free_energy = -kT * np.log(unbiased_hist)
    free_energy -= {{b3}}
    return bin_centers, free_energy

questions.title: VAMP Score for MSM Validation
questions.description: Compute the VAMP-2 score, a variational measure of how well a featurization or model captures the slow dynamics of a molecular system. The VAMP score is based on the singular values of the time-lagged covariance matrix.
questions.info: The Variational Approach for Markov Processes (VAMP) score (Wu & Noe, J Nonlinear Sci 2020) provides a rigorous variational principle for evaluating and optimizing models of molecular kinetics. The VAMP-2 score equals the sum of squared singular values of the Koopman operator approximation and is maximized by the true slow modes. It serves as an objective function for training deep learning models like VAMPnets (Mardt et al., Nat Commun 2018) that learn optimal reaction coordinates directly from simulation data. Unlike the MSM implied timescales, the VAMP score provides a single scalar metric for model comparison. Higher VAMP-2 scores indicate better capture of the slow dynamical processes. Reference: Wu & Noe, J Nonlinear Sci 2020; Mardt et al., Nat Commun 2018.

import numpy as np

def vamp2_score(features, lag=10):
    """Compute the VAMP-2 score from a feature trajectory.
    features: (T, D) feature trajectory
    lag: time lag in frames
    Returns: VAMP-2 score (scalar)
    """
    T, D = features.shape
    # Split into time-lagged pairs
    X = features[:T - lag]  # (T-lag, D)
    Y = features[{{b1}}:]   # (T-lag, D)
    # Center
    X = X - X.mean(axis=0)
    Y = Y - Y.mean(axis=0)
    n = X.shape[0]
    # Covariance matrices
    C00 = X.T @ X / n
    C11 = Y.T @ Y / n
    C01 = X.T @ Y / n
    # Regularize
    C00 += 1e-6 * np.eye(D)
    C11 += 1e-6 * np.eye(D)
    # VAMP-2 score: ||C00^{-1/2} C01 C11^{-1/2}||_F^2
    C00_inv_sqrt = np.linalg.inv({{b2}})
    C11_inv_sqrt = np.linalg.inv(np.linalg.cholesky(C11))
    M = C00_inv_sqrt @ C01 @ C11_inv_sqrt.T
    singular_values = np.linalg.svd(M, compute_uv=False)
    return np.sum({{b3}})

T, D = 10000, 10
# Create features with one slow mode
t = np.arange(T)
slow = np.sin(2 * np.pi * t / 1000)[:, None]  # slow oscillation
fast = np.random.randn(T, D - 1) * 0.5
