score = vamp2_score(features, lag=50)
print(f'VAMP-2 score: {score:.4f}')

blanks.hint: The time-lagged data starts at index 'lag'.
Use Cholesky decomposition of C00 to compute its inverse square root.
The VAMP-2 score is the sum of squared singular values.

questions.title: PCA of MD Trajectory (Essential Dynamics)
questions.description: Perform PCA on an MD trajectory to identify the dominant modes of motion (essential dynamics). Center the trajectory, compute the covariance matrix, and project onto the top principal components.
questions.info: Principal Component Analysis (PCA) of MD trajectories, also called Essential Dynamics (Amadei et al., Proteins 1993), identifies the dominant collective motions by diagonalizing the covariance matrix of atomic fluctuations. The top principal components typically capture large-amplitude functional motions in only a few degrees of freedom. Unlike NMA which assumes a harmonic potential, PCA captures anharmonic motions present in the simulation. PCA is often used as a preprocessing step before MSM construction, though TICA (Perez-Hernandez et al., J Chem Phys 2013) is preferred when slow modes are the target. MDAnalysis (Michaud-Agrawal et al., J Comput Chem 2011) implements trajectory PCA. The explained variance ratio indicates how many PCs are needed to capture the essential motions. Reference: Amadei et al., Proteins 1993; Michaud-Agrawal et al., J Comput Chem 2011.
questions.code:
import numpy as np

def trajectory_pca(traj, n_components=3):
    """PCA of an MD trajectory.
    traj: (T, N, 3) trajectory array
    n_components: number of PCs to retain
    Returns: projections (T, n_components), explained_variance_ratio
    """
    T, N, _ = traj.shape
    # Flatten spatial dimensions
    X = traj.reshape(T, {{b1}})  # (T, 3N)
    # Center
    X_centered = X - X.mean(axis=0)
    # Covariance matrix
    cov = X_centered.T @ X_centered / (T - 1)
    # Eigendecomposition
    eigenvalues, eigenvectors = np.linalg.eigh(cov)
    # Sort descending
    idx = np.argsort(eigenvalues)[::-1]
    eigenvalues = eigenvalues[idx]
    eigenvectors = eigenvectors[:, idx]
    # Project onto top PCs
    projections = X_centered @ eigenvectors[:, {{b2}}]
    explained = eigenvalues[:n_components] / {{b3}}
    return projections, explained

np.random.seed(42)
T, N = 2000, 50
traj = np.random.randn(T, N, 3) * 0.1
# Add a dominant mode
traj += np.sin(np.linspace(0, 4*np.pi, T))[:, None, None] * np.random.randn(N, 3)
proj, var_ratio = trajectory_pca(traj, n_components=3)
print('Projection shape:', proj.shape)
print('Explained variance ratios:', var_ratio)

blanks.hint: Flatten each frame from (N, 3) to a single vector of length 3N.
Select the first n_components eigenvectors for projection.
Divide by the sum of all eigenvalues to get the fraction of variance explained.

questions.title: SO(3)-Equivariant Linear Layer for Spherical Harmonics
questions.description: Implement an SO(3)-equivariant linear layer that mixes features of the same angular momentum order l. For equivariance, the learnable weight matrix can only mix channels within the same l, not across different l values.
questions.info: Equivariant neural networks for molecular property prediction use spherical harmonics as basis functions for representing geometric features. An SO(3)-equivariant linear layer can only mix features within the same irreducible representation (same angular momentum l), because cross-l mixing would break equivariance (Thomas et al., arXiv 2018). For each l, the layer applies an independent learnable weight matrix to the channel dimension while leaving the 2l+1 magnetic quantum number dimension untouched. This is a core building block of TFN (Thomas et al., arXiv 2018), NequIP (Batzner et al., Nat Commun 2022), and MACE. The e3nn library (Geiger & Smidt, arXiv 2022) provides production-ready implementations of these equivariant layers. Reference: Thomas et al., arXiv 2018; Geiger & Smidt, arXiv 2022.
questions.code:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn

class EquivariantLinear(nn.Module):
    """SO(3)-equivariant linear layer for multi-l features."""
    def __init__(self, l_max, channels_in, channels_out):
        super().__init__()
        self.l_max = l_max
        # One weight matrix per angular momentum order
        self.weights = nn.ParameterList([
            nn.Parameter(torch.randn(channels_out, channels_in) * 0.01)
            for _ in range({{b1}})
        ])

    def forward(self, features_by_l):
        """features_by_l: list of tensors, one per l.
        features_by_l[l] has shape (N, channels_in, 2*l+1)
        Returns: list of (N, channels_out, 2*l+1)
        """
        outputs = []
        for l, (feat, W) in enumerate(zip(features_by_l, self.weights)):
            # feat: (N, channels_in, 2l+1), W: (channels_out, channels_in)
            # Mix channels only, preserve the 2l+1 dimension
            out = torch.einsum('oi, nim -> {{b2}}', W, feat)
            outputs.append(out)
        return outputs

N, c_in, c_out, l_max = 32, 16, 8, 2
model = EquivariantLinear(l_max, c_in, c_out)
# l=0: scalars (2*0+1=1), l=1: vectors (3), l=2: rank-2 (5)
features = [torch.randn(N, c_in, 2*l+1) for l in range(l_max + 1)]
outputs = model(features)
for l, out in enumerate(outputs):
    expected_m = {{b3}}
    print(f'l={l}: shape {out.shape}, expected m-dim: {expected_m}')

blanks.hint: We need one weight matrix for each l from 0 to l_max inclusive.
The output has shape (N, channels_out, 2l+1): contract over input channels i.
The magnetic quantum number dimension for angular momentum l has size 2l+1.

questions.title: AlphaFold Confidence: pLDDT and PAE Matrix
questions.description: Given AlphaFold's raw logits for pLDDT bins and Predicted Aligned Error (PAE) bins, convert them to the actual pLDDT score and PAE distance matrix. These confidence metrics guide interpretation of AlphaFold predictions.
questions.info: AlphaFold2 (Jumper et al., Nature 2021) outputs per-residue pLDDT as a confidence score and the Predicted Aligned Error (PAE) matrix for assessing inter-domain accuracy. pLDDT is predicted as a distribution over distance bins and converted to a score by computing the expected lDDT. The PAE matrix element (i,j) estimates the error in residue j's position when aligning on residue i's frame. PAE is especially valuable for AlphaFold-Multimer (Evans et al., bioRxiv 2022) predictions, where low inter-chain PAE indicates confident interface prediction. pLDDT > 90 and PAE < 5 Angstroms generally indicate reliable predictions. These metrics are critical for filtering and interpreting structural models. Reference: Jumper et al., Nature 2021; Evans et al., bioRxiv 2022.
questions.code:
import numpy as np
from scipy.special import softmax

def compute_plddt(plddt_logits):
    """Convert pLDDT logits to scores.
    plddt_logits: (L, 50) logits over 50 lDDT bins
    Returns: (L,) pLDDT scores in [0, 100]
    """
    # Bin centers: 0.5, 1.5, ..., 49.5 mapped to 0-100
    bin_centers = np.arange(0.5, 50.0, 1.0) * {{b1}}  # scale to 0-100
    probs = softmax(plddt_logits, axis=-1)  # (L, 50)
    plddt = np.sum(probs * bin_centers, axis=-1)  # expected value
    return plddt

def compute_pae(pae_logits, max_dist=31.75):
    """Convert PAE logits to distance matrix.
    pae_logits: (L, L, 64) logits over 64 distance bins
    Returns: (L, L) predicted aligned error in Angstroms
    """
    bin_centers = np.linspace(0.25, max_dist, 64)
    probs = softmax(pae_logits, axis={{b2}})
    pae = np.sum(probs * bin_centers, axis=-1)
    return pae

np.random.seed(42)
L = 100
plddt_logits = np.random.randn(L, 50)
pae_logits = np.random.randn(L, L, 64)
plddt = compute_plddt(plddt_logits)
pae = compute_pae(pae_logits)
print('pLDDT shape:', plddt.shape, 'range:', plddt.min(), '-', plddt.max())
print('PAE shape:', pae.shape, 'mean:', pae.mean())
print('PAE is not symmetric:', not np.allclose(pae, pae.{{b3}}))

blanks.hint: Scale 50 bins to cover 0-100: multiply by 2 (or 100/50).
Apply softmax over the last axis (the bin dimension).
PAE is generally not symmetric because the alignment frame differs for (i,j) vs (j,i).

questions.title: DiffDock Translation Diffusion Step
questions.description: Implement the forward diffusion step for the translational component of DiffDock. The ligand center of mass undergoes Brownian motion on R^3, adding Gaussian noise at each diffusion timestep with a variance schedule.
questions.info: DiffDock (Corso et al., ICLR 2023) models molecular docking as a generative diffusion process over the product space SE(3) x Torsion, decomposed into translation (R^3), rotation (SO(3)), and torsion angle components. The translational diffusion follows a standard variance-preserving schedule where Gaussian noise is added to the ligand center of mass. The score model learns to reverse this diffusion to generate binding poses from noise. The noise schedule is critical for training stability and is typically parameterized by a monotonically increasing sigma(t). During inference, the reverse diffusion iteratively denoises random poses into predicted binding configurations. The decomposition into independent diffusion processes for each degree of freedom simplifies the score matching objective. Reference: Corso et al., ICLR 2023; Ho et al., NeurIPS 2020.
questions.code:
import torch
import numpy as np

def translation_diffusion_forward(ligand_coords, t, sigma_schedule):
    """Apply forward translational diffusion to ligand.
    ligand_coords: (N_atoms, 3) ligand atom coordinates
    t: diffusion timestep (0 to 1)
    sigma_schedule: function mapping t -> noise level sigma
    Returns: noisy_coords (N_atoms, 3), noise (3,) applied to center of mass
    """
    sigma = sigma_schedule(t)
    # Sample translation noise
    tr_noise = torch.randn(3) * {{b1}}
    # Apply same translation to all atoms (rigid body)
    noisy_coords = ligand_coords + {{b2}}
    return noisy_coords, tr_noise

def sigma_schedule(t, sigma_min=0.1, sigma_max=10.0):
    """Log-linear noise schedule."""
    return sigma_min * (sigma_max / sigma_min) ** t

# Test
torch.manual_seed(42)
ligand = torch.randn(15, 3)  # 15-atom ligand
orig_com = ligand.mean(dim=0)
noisy, noise = translation_diffusion_forward(ligand, t=0.5, sigma_schedule=sigma_schedule)
new_com = noisy.mean(dim=0)
# All atoms should be shifted by the same amount
atom_shifts = noisy - ligand
print('All atoms shifted equally:', torch.allclose(atom_shifts[0], atom_shifts[{{b3}}]))
print('COM shift:', (new_com - orig_com).numpy())
print('Sigma at t=0.5:', sigma_schedule(0.5))

blanks.hint: Scale the random noise by sigma to control the noise level at this timestep.
Add the same translational noise to all atoms (broadcast the 3D noise vector).
Pick any other atom index to verify all atoms received the same rigid-body translation.

questions.title: Contact Order: Topological Complexity of a Fold
questions.description: Compute the relative contact order of a protein from its contact map. Contact order measures the average sequence separation of contacting residue pairs and correlates with protein folding rate.
questions.info: Relative contact order (Plaxco et al., J Mol Biol 1998) is defined as the average sequence separation of all residue pairs in contact, divided by the protein length. It is one of the strongest predictors of two-state protein folding rates: proteins with lower contact order (more local contacts, like helical proteins) fold faster than those with higher contact order (more non-local contacts, like beta-sheet proteins). Contact order can be computed from either experimental structures or predicted structures from AlphaFold2 (Jumper et al., Nature 2021). The metric ranges from ~5% for simple helical bundles to ~20% for complex beta-sheet topologies. It provides a simple topological descriptor of protein architecture. Reference: Plaxco et al., J Mol Biol 1998; Ivankov et al., Protein Sci 2003.
questions.code:
import numpy as np

def relative_contact_order(contact_map):
    """Compute relative contact order from a binary contact map.
    contact_map: (L, L) binary contact matrix
    Returns: relative contact order (scalar)
    """
    L = contact_map.shape[0]
    # Get indices of contacting pairs (upper triangle only)
    i_idx, j_idx = np.where(np.triu(contact_map, k={{b1}}))
    if len(i_idx) == 0:
        return 0.0
    # Sequence separations
    seq_seps = {{b2}}
    # Relative contact order
    co = np.mean(seq_seps) / {{b3}}
    return co

np.random.seed(42)
L = 100
ca_coords = np.random.randn(L, 3) * 10
dist = np.linalg.norm(ca_coords[:, None] - ca_coords[None, :], axis=-1)
contact_map = (dist < 8.0).astype(float)
np.fill_diagonal(contact_map, 0)
co = relative_contact_order(contact_map)
print(f'Relative contact order: {co:.4f}')
print(f'Number of contacts: {int(np.triu(contact_map, k=1).sum())}')

blanks.hint: Use k=1 to exclude the diagonal (self-contacts) and get only upper triangle.
Sequence separation is the absolute difference between residue indices.
Divide by the protein length L to get the relative (normalized) contact order.
